word.item: marsh, slough; cove, bay, inlet, pond
marsh ( for rice ), cove,bay, inlet, swamp, pond ( v & j )
Stream of the Cranes (Coulée des Grues) goes to Old River and from there to Red River
in the lowlands, in the marshes
there is no water pond ( J ) ; Gatsch. gives this in a slightly different form as the equilavent of " vuphar, thistle-down, etc," but this certainly wrong
to dress wood ( with an ax, etc. )
I dress wood (as to square up a sill)
piece of wood dressed with the hatchet
they go out ( of the water )
outlet, mouth " bayou's going out into a lake "
an implement for making sharp points on the sugar cane ( S )
arrow-head point ( G ) something you sharpen with a knife ( V )
I am pressing or squeezing something
bile gall, " full of yellow " ( G ) ( prob. wrong, J )
the man who owns a cotton-gin
to finish, accomplish, eud, complete, become
a child being born, or that has just been born
rib " a flat sided human bone "
this thing is flat, level ( like a table )
this tree is as high as the house, on a level with the house
although he is lame, he walks well
a ladder to go up by; staircase ( V)
to cut wood across ( the grain )
I am cutting cloth, a yard measure
a New Year's greeting, has some reference to some kind of big day
Look you! (expt. of atonishment) Let us look! (v)
I bend backward, evidently, " I see upward" (in bending over backward)
I stop someone, prob. " I have spoken to someone to stop him"
to crackle, crack, burst, break wind
the or sprouts on a tree (G) ( Johnson says " to pass by a tree", evidently deriving the vb. from he'xku) ( ? )
to open itself, to break out, unfold
soft rd. obj. , bull, india-rubber pincushion
corn mush (puri always occurs withh prceding complement)
to swell, inflate or distend by blowing
virgin, "woman with a hard or firm heart"
the water is hardened, i.e. frozen
to comb, also to paint; comb (m.)
to enclose, to catch by enclosing
I am constipated, "backsides to me closed"
woman who has had a child
I am beginning to play ball
pick-ax, flat spoon (G.; V. & J. do recognize this)
I flatten the head of a child
leggings (of buckskin) used in old times
I flash (a promise) with a torch
I am going with a torch
I have brought him (a child) up (non-reverential form)
a place spoiled by being grown over with grass and bushes
ropes used to fasten down the lower part of a tent (J.)
corn-husks, the chaff of corn, fodder
I put it inside of (the earth)
having planted 4 wooden pickets to whip a negro, they whipped him
cord to fasten bales of cotton
tree with plenty of fruit or acorns
on this side of the stone
(f) I have the diarrhea (names of diseases are feminine)
to sleep, sleep; used in speaking of death; also instead of a´xcuk, day, to indicate a period 24 hours
they (people) did not know him (e´rusa to know)
the cat climbs down from the tree (stem honu)
galaxy, "dog trail" (G. says te´ti rowa "white trail")
a necklace composed of silver pieces ([?]. and J.), Gatschet says "bone beads" but appears to be wrong
eating, possibly also the name for an animal
we let us eat (we is an exclusive)
I paste or glue; I screw, I put together
paste, glue, to put the button there
I am making a roast, or I am cooking something
he wagered himself to the alligator outside
cold (seem to have become slightly differentiated from one root)
I cool myself down; (and "let me brag of myself")
when they have not cooled (the fever) off
I tremble with cold or shiver
a tempest, stormy weather, "an assemblage of rain storms"
to stretch a hide, or a stretched or drawn hide
until the day dawned for her
my paternal uncle, my father's youngest brother
I have begun to dry up
if I am treated + not cured there is no payment
trunk of a tree as far as the branches, body of the tree
I have or had been strong (J.), something coming behind that is very strong (V.)
an old Indian game in which two marked pieces of wood were shuffled up in cotton + bets made on which sides they would have uppermost
to be satisfied, pleased, to thank
I thank him; I have made him glad (V.)
the rapids in Red Rive in Alexandria, fn.
that pained him; hes was sad (V.)
a male infant, a little boy (V.)
the sweat of a man or human being
to deliver the soul of the dead; we save our souls (V.)
I make a hole thru a rock
to prick or sting; to point; to fasten (ef. tcuxpu)
pain or stitches in the side (?)
big round silver pieces worn on breast
to set a dog on one
poison, venom, what disturbs one; to fast, to mourn
I fast, I take poison (i.e, medicine)
creature, animal, "small cattle", used for caterpillar
"white creature", a white worm living in the ground
screech owl (has a cry like a little chicken, feathery ears, 4-5 in., flies at night
I am eating several kinds of food together, as bread and meat, or I am eating bread and something with it (J.)
to tremble with fear, fever, etc., to fear
posteriors, buttocks of man (males) and animals
pounded or crushed rice, pounding rice in a mortar (V.)
a man who can keep his own council and not tell things to everyone (V.), a discreet man
the oval of the face, my face is round
the antennae of the crab, "crab's eyelids"
I have not time to come
a blackguard, a man with bad ways
this boy has shot his arrow straight up
maynard's or wild apple, Creole aconu
a red-headed lizard (?), or something similar (V.)
red paint, vermilion, "red stuff blown on" (so called because the vermilion was blown upon the face)
prob. this should be considered a participial suff. tho it often appears like a suffixt conjunction
she having climbed fell back many times when halfway up
to plant, to place, something put down
a wooden picket planted or placed
the green fly-catcher or kingbird; Creole Moquer grosse tête
to put a pot on the fire
I begin to split a rail
the rattle made by a rattlesnake, "the noise made by a rattlesnake when he is angry" (V. & J.)
to complain, to whine when feeling badly
kinky & curly hair like that of the whites
2nd chief (many); when called tco´ha ta´waka commands in war. tawa´ka axkicikun (man appointed by the tco´ha); a leader in anything, governor
to fall in drops, to drop, to drip
I am making the water drip down
I am sucking something, or I draw something (V.)
1) I draw or bleed, said only of the little horn used by doctors in sucking out disease, 2) I embrace
I spit down in front of myself
bat (pa´hala, wings; tuk little) (tcuk is employed only in this word)
(m.) fox (the same in Creek, Choctaw, & Cherokee)
long-tailed wild cat; Creole chat tigre
I take him out (of water, etc.)
they drew (him) out of the water
stairs; a prop to the door or steps (J.)
the side-pieces of the ladder; one tree lying against another
a roll (of paper as a cigarette, etc.), cylindrical
they (m.), (subj. pron. suff. of 3 pl. m.)
I flatten the head of a child (ta above)
to put on the right side
they go in cuneus (like wild geese)
general definite article prefixed to nouns & infinitives
to slide or glide, slippery; to reflect
enough & no more, only, nothing other
with, while, during, in the manner of; (see examples)
I go about (J.); I do not know where I'm going (V.)
harvest, "when you want to plant your crop" (tapa, vegetables, what is planted)
I salute him by shaking hands
fold of skin under lizard's chin that moves when he turns head
(m.) lizard (tci´ya refers to its jumping; ta´xkani as above)
his shoulder-blade (of man, ox, hog, etc.)
Bayou Choctaw, or Bayou de l'Île (at the eastern end of Bayou Lamourie, and so called because it runs around 2 islands)
noun probably, "at the side, at one side"
to put on one side, as if fixing onself up on only one side
patched places, to patch, things patched by placing their ends together
their canoes joined end to end, i.e. like rafters
soul, shade, reflection; shade of a tree
(a blue bird which is blue on the back but yellow on the belly; it comes in the spring -- G.) this is probably a mistake for me´lin, the full form being o´xcta me´lin
the sounding or ringing of the bell
silver ornaments held or jointed at one end
appears to be sign of plural
next year, in the early spring (V.)
Into what villages are you going?
the side toward the north, northward, north
toward the place where I am standing
the threshhold of the door, "placed across the door," cross-piece on a door
ridge of nose; ridge of house (ri) (V.)
fine hominy, "cut corn" (?), Creole petit gru
arrow-head (G.); something one sharpens with a knife (V.)
I do not have a ball
What is the name of the man who is passing?
hominy with something put into it, hominy cooked with a hog's head, soup
given by Gatschet at "the bottom of the lake" but probably "something lying in a lake" would be more correct
Bayou Lamourie (G.), Bayou de La Source (V. & G.); might be applied to any bayou (J.)
it rains and turns to sleet, it sleets (V.)
an icicle (V.), a coasting of very slippery ice (G.)
it is necessary to throw on the cemetery; to put something in a grave
to put something into hominy, hominy with something else put into it
to go off, explore (as a gun)
"little diver", a diving bird of medium size, the male a deep purple with white head, the female gray; probably the widgeon (G.)
I am fatigued, I'm getting tired
prismatic wood with six faces (G.), six round sticks (J.)
prismatic wood with 8 faces (G.), eith round sticks
he hurls himself or bumps against the door
I bend something, I plant it leaning over
father-in-law, father of wife or husband, step-father (?V.)
up on a hill, a hillside or bank (f. timura)
the great horned owl; Creole hibon groose tête
I put the cows in the corral
inhabitant, let us go into the house
the dwelling, the house for sitting down
I am going to sit down
a blot, "dirt stain"; a spot of dirt (J.)
I watch (in speaking of the dead, being alone to watch)
he had put him (in a house to fast)
a good ball for a gun
a hoe (J.), the spleen, milt (G.), "spoon flat"?
probably the same as above, pickax (G.)
to whinny like a horse or crow like a cock
an old not lively toad, perhaps "frog"
I raise and throw away the cover
she wanted to throw him away
to weep, cry, to crow, also the bellowing of a bull, singing of a bird
they do not light up a dead person
on each (bank), opposite each other; open on each side (V.)
opposite the stone, opposite an open place with a stone on it
path, the turning row in a field
the night will never come, it will never be night
this man will never come or attend
he has ordained or commanded them
their custom; they have been commanded
a cemetery (?), "a decayed hole"; "a grave that has cave in" (V.)
land spoiled by being run over with bushes, etc.
to break up; rough to the touch
springing upward after the arrow he overtook it (he sat on it)
I am hopping on one leg
"to pull out stopper from bottle", corkscrew
to pull out a person's tooth
feverish, one sick of the stone, one who has a fever
the burner of souls; applied 1) to God as punisher, 2) to the devil
inflammation of the stomach (V.); wanting a young person (J.)
ancient times, old times, centuries back
a polypod which grows on bark of greenwood, moss (?), "hunting medicine or drink"
my buckskin trousers are ripped along the seams
he went half a mile, or he got half a mile
(m.) "concealer of the army," a beetle or bug
the little blue heron; Creole un gru
to be economical, to be careful, pay attention to something
I do not waste, I am economical
I pay attention, I come slowly, i.e. carefully
a canal, a ditch occupied by a bayou
to hear, to listen, to understand
to scratch (with such an object as a needle, as was done by doctors in olden times, so as to draw blood)
I am playing or joking with somebody (V. and J.), I am saucy to somebody (G.)
they joke with me, or play a joke on me (V. and J.), they are saucy to me (G.)
elastic like a marsh, which buldges, is swollen
"elm drink or medicine," (Gatschet gives this as the name of the slippery elm, but Johnson says it is diifferent)
count, account, to count, to relate
I count, I am relating to him
you (f.) go and ask for, demand him
"climbing on the bear"(?), water snipe (V. and J.), woodcock (G.); Creole becasse de nuits(?)
I cover a pot, or pitcher, a cover
cover of tin bucket, "white metal cup cover"
they envelop or cover him (in a cotton cloth)
striped in a serpentine manner, undulating
to urinate (same word for all persons)
I am showing, making, see, I am teaching
to give to eat, to feed
we feed, raise, or domesticate hens
the duck plunges in going in swimming
the fish swims with its fins
cut on the bias (?), oblique(?), "cut across points"
they go in "cuneus", or in a point like wild geese
prefix indicating that one person or thing reaches another from the point of view of the latter, i.e. "to arrive coming"
to make, to do, cause to be
antelope, "little deer dwelling in the palmettos"
word used in forming numeral adverbs
picture of a monkey (J.); imprint or coinage ("melted portrait"); picture of a limb
crane, heron (perhaps meaning "to come" (from the south))
the white or American egret; Creole egret
(f.) medicine for poisoning (perhaps this he hî´ran ta´yaxpu, -- see ya´xpu)
they go in single file, one by one
to poison (there appears to be some confusion between this and ya´xku q.v.)
"owl poison", oak way, mistletoe (?); Creole, des gee or des zee
the entire mixture, all mixed together
sick; to be angry; pain grief
Lally thinks that she is sick
he is to wear fine clothing
a fashionable or well dressed man
I put my fine clothes on, I plume myself
then, now, there (cf. hin and ka)
a sorcerer, sorceress, wizard, witch, evil person
a sorceress, a woman who does evil
I made up my mind right away (V.?)
he made up his mind right away (V.?)
I stumble about and try to catch myself to keep from falling (J.)
prefix indicating that one person or thing reaches another from the point of view of the former's starting point, i.e. "to arrive going"
the jack fish (?); Creole la grande greule or "sacale"; Gatschet gives it as salmon, but is probably wrong
many, a lot of (cf. preceding stem)
